text: The surgeon uses two forceps to retract the fundus of the gallbladder, creating space and revealing adhesions of the omentum formed due to inflammation over time.
The surgeon tears away the tissue layer beneath which the gallbladder is located, revealing lymph nodes on the right-hand side inferiorly.
The surgeon tears off the peritoneum, revealing underlying structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon touches the tip of the telescope to the liver, a solid organ, to avoid thermal injury to hollow organs. The lymph node of the cystic artery is visible on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a standard Calot's triangle dissection using Maryland curved forceps, carefully dissecting between the cystic duct and the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes red adhesions indicating inflammation, a visual sign of redness that is atypical in this area.
The surgeon dissects close to the gallbladder, carefully navigating fibrous adhesions to avoid the underlying common hepatic duct.
The surgeon dissects above the cystic duct, revealing the anatomical structures beneath the gallbladder, including the common hepatic duct, portal vein, hepatic artery, and liver.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection on the right side of the surgical field, combining both anterior and posterior approaches to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon carefully dissects fibro-fatty and fibrinous adhesions near the infundibulum of the gallbladder, approaching its junction with the cystic duct.
The surgeon exposes the common bile duct, formed at the junction of the common hepatic duct and cystic duct, visible below the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes the anatomical continuity between the common hepatic duct and the common bile duct, noting the potential for adherence to pull these structures closer to the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully separates the dense fibro-fatty and fibrinous adhesions around the gallbladder, using a slow and gradual technique to avoid damaging nearby structures.
The surgeon gently delineates the anatomy around the infundibulum of the gallbladder and its junction with the cystic duct, carefully separating fibro-fatty and fibrinous adhesions.

The surgeon carefully removes the peritoneum from the lymph node near the cystic duct, using gentle traction to avoid tearing the tissue. The dissection is performed anteriorly on the left side, with minimal pressure applied to prevent damage.

The surgeon encounters resistance while attempting to separate tissue planes, opting to leave the area and approach from the opposite side. The pulling and stretching gradually open up the tissue planes.
The surgeon identifies the posterior branch of the cystic artery without applying traction, ensuring careful handling of the vessel.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the gallbladder, avoiding strong pulling to prevent tearing and bleeding. The anatomy reveals the 'elephant trunk sign,' where the gallbladder tapers into the cystic duct, which is visibly small.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, which visually resembles the cystic duct, and performs careful dissection above it to confirm that the duct is entering the gallbladder rather than the liver.
The surgeon carefully separates a lymph node during the dissection process, ensuring precision to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node overlying the cystic artery. The lymph node is being carefully dissected and removed to expose the cystic artery beneath it.
The surgeon carefully removes tissue supplied by the cystic artery, avoiding excessive pressure to prevent tearing and retraction of the artery, which could lead to uncontrolled bleeding.
The surgeon attempts to pull apart the tissue but stops when resistance is encountered, returning to the dissection on the right side and inferiorly.
The surgeon uses traction to open up tissue planes, allowing for the dissection of the gallbladder away from the gallbladder bed or hepatic fossa, where it is situated.

The surgeon performs a semi-retrograde dissection, starting from above and moving downward between the gallbladder and liver. This technique creates a clear plane, avoiding ducts and arteries located below.
The surgeon approaches the vessels and ducts, including bile canals, identifying structures to be preserved and those to be cut.

The surgeon identifies polycystohepatic vessels supplying the gallbladder, noting that these small vessels originate from the gallbladder bed. The cystic artery is observed to supply both the gallbladder and potentially a portion of the liver.

The surgeon uses the left hand to mobilize the gallbladder away from the liver bed, carefully hugging the gallbladder to stay close to it and avoid the common hepatic duct. The dissection progresses as the surgeon visually confirms proximity to the gallbladder and builds a plane between the gallbladder and liver.

The surgeon opens a space just under the gallbladder, carefully mobilizing it away from the liver bed. The dissection creates a plane between the gallbladder and liver, ensuring the common hepatic duct remains undisturbed.
The surgeon opens up the space close to the gallbladder, revealing a small duct and fibrous strand in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully assesses the posterior branch of the cystic artery, deciding against shearing it off due to potential bleeding risks. A clip applicator is brought into view, and a clip is applied to the vessel to prevent bleeding into the foramen of Winslow.
The surgeon identifies and cuts the posterior branch of the cystic artery, which is not commonly present. A clip is applied to prevent bleeding from the vessel.
The surgeon observes a small bleeding vessel on the right side and decides to apply another clip to control the bleeding, avoiding the use of electrocautery.
The surgeon places a clip on a vessel to prevent collateral damage, then cuts distal to the clip. The dissection progresses towards achieving the critical view of safety.
The surgeon achieves a clear critical view of safety, revealing the common bile duct, common hepatic duct, and cystic artery. Clips are applied at the base of the cystic artery.
The surgeon applies pressure on the clip to ensure it is securely fastened to the cystic artery, preventing it from slipping.

The surgeon assesses the cystic duct, noting its larger size compared to the current clip. A larger clip is introduced and successfully applied to the cystic duct, with the tip of the clip applicator visible on the opposite side.

The surgeon applies the first clip onto the thick cystic duct, ensuring it is pressed firmly to prevent slipping. The clip is checked for secure application, with adjustments made if necessary to ensure the duct remains tightly closed.

The surgeon applies two proximal clips on the cystic duct and prepares to apply a distal clip to prevent stone and bile spillage. The cystic duct is carefully divided between the clips, ensuring the common bile duct and common hepatic duct remain intact. A small vessel is clipped posteriorly, and the cystic artery is divided, with visible pulsation observed in the artery.

The surgeon uses a unipolar cautery hook to address anatomical anomalies in the area, including unnamed vessels coming from various directions, after the gallbladder has been mobilized and vital structures have been secured.

The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, gently dissecting fibrous adhesions between the liver and gallbladder. Gallstones are visible through the semi-transparent gallbladder wall. The surgeon checks the clips on the cystic duct and artery, preparing to remove the gallbladder.
